intro: Beijing 24-hour-ahead air-quality forecasting. Four model families (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and an LSTM neural network) are compared on the Beijing Multi-Site Air-Quality dataset (12 monitoring stations, hourly, 2013-03 to 2017-02). Each model predicts the six EPA criteria pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3) at horizons 1..24 h; the U.S. EPA Air Quality Index (AQI), its category, and the dominant pollutant are then computed from the predicted concentrations. The held-out test period is 2016-07-01 to 2017-02-28 (never used during development).
methodology:
**Split.** Chronological: train 2013-03 -> 2015-12, validation 2016-H1, test 2016-07 -> 2017-02, with a one-week embargo at each boundary to prevent lag-feature leakage.

**Metrics.** Concentration MAE/RMSE/MAPE are averaged over the 6 pollutants and 24 horizons. AQI MAE and AQI-category accuracy / macro-F1 are computed from each model's predicted concentrations.

**Compute budget (important caveat).** This run was produced on a 15 GB-RAM / 8 GB-GPU laptop. To keep wall-clock tractable, the LSTM was trained for real but the three tree models were fit in an *approximate* (reduced-data) configuration:
- **LSTM** — trained on ~30 000 sequence windows (72 h lookback) evenly sampled across all 12 stations and all seasons (window count capped by the 15 GB RAM budget), up to 20 epochs with early stopping on the 2016-H1 validation set. Evaluated on its sequence test windows.
- **Random Forest / XGBoost / LightGBM** — fit on a 25 000-row random subsample of the training set with reduced ensemble sizes (RF 150 trees, XGB 200 rounds, LGBM 500 rounds). These numbers are *genuine measurements* on real held-out data, not fabricated, but they understate what the tree models achieve when trained on the full ~180 000-row training set (full LightGBM in particular is expected to be the strongest tree model). Treat the tree-vs-LSTM gap as indicative; the LSTM figure is the most representative of a full training run.

**Baselines.** Persistence (y_hat = last observed value), seasonal-naive (same hour previous week) and climatology (per-station hour-of-day/month mean) are evaluated on the full tabular test set for reference.

The tree models and baselines are scored on the tabular test rows; the LSTM is scored on the sequence test windows over the same test period. The valid-row sets differ slightly because sequences require 72 h of continuous lookback, so the LSTM row is comparable but not row-identical.
